title: For Democracy to Function Properly!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored from the printed page, and clear OCR errors in figures, words, spacing, and line joins have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Mr Sandeep Saxena, IAS, formerly Secretary of the Agriculture Department, has been transferred and has assumed office as Tamil Nadu's Chief Electoral Officer. He has given his first interview to reporters. On behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, I congratulate him on taking up this important responsibility.

In that interview he said that anyone who turns eighteen by 1 January 2015 may add their name to the electoral roll. A special enrolment drive is being conducted from 15 October to 10 November. Applications may be obtained and submitted on working days at 64,092 polling stations across Tamil Nadu. Since working people cannot visit on office days, special camps have also been arranged on the holidays of Sunday, 26 October and Sunday, 2 November, at 27,000 centres containing polling stations. Only those whose names appear on the roll can vote. People should visit their nearest station on Sunday, verify their name, and enrol if it is absent.

It is entirely wrong to imagine that election work begins only when the date or schedule is announced, or only when someone is named the party's official candidate. Adding new voters and checking the roll are indispensable tasks that must begin many months earlier.

Before every election, once the Election Commission issues its notice, party headquarters duly releases a statement asking DMK colleagues to begin enrolment work immediately, and Murasoli publishes it prominently. But party colleagues must not remain idle thinking, “That is only the candidate's work,” or, “It has nothing to do with us.”

The Commission had already announced enrolment in Tamil Nadu from 15 October to 10 November. Party headquarters issued an appeal, and our brother Murasoli Selvam published a boxed item titled “Warning! Warning!” asking colleagues to act immediately. Yet many days later, I do not see sufficient participation by our Udanpirappugal. Meanwhile I receive reports that rival parties, including ministers themselves, are enrolling new voters and engaging in irregular attempts to remove from the roll the names of people publicly known as important DMK members.

The Hindu carried an important report on 25 April 2014 comparing the rise in voter numbers since the previous election. Uttar Pradesh rose from 11.60 crore voters in 2009 to 13.44 crore in 2014, an increase of 15.8 per cent. Bihar rose from 5.45 crore to 6.21 crore, or 13.9 per cent. Maharashtra rose from 7.30 crore to 7.90 crore, an increase of 8.2 per cent. Andhra Pradesh rose from 5.79 crore to 6.24 crore, or 7.8 per cent. Do you know what happened in Tamil Nadu?
Tamil Nadu had 4,16,20,460 voters in 2009. By 2014 this had risen to 5,37,52,682, an increase of 29.1 per cent.
Uttar Pradesh: 15.8 per cent. Bihar: 13.9 per cent. Maharashtra: 8.2 per cent. Andhra Pradesh: 7.8 per cent. Karnataka: 6.9 per cent. But Tamil Nadu alone: 29.1 per cent. What caused this?

In 1999 Tamil Nadu had 4.77 crore voters. In 2004 it had 4.72 crore, a decline. By 2009 the number had fallen further to 4.16 crore, a twelve per cent drop in five years. Yet instead of continuing to decline, it rose by 29.1 per cent in 2014. What does that mean? Why did new enrolment grow so enormously? So many new voters were added before the 2014 parliamentary election. Does the evidence not indicate that this enrolment helped the AIADMK win thirty-seven seats?

By the 2011 Assembly election, after the 2009 parliamentary election, new enrolment had increased by about twenty per cent. At the 2014 parliamentary election, it rose by another ten percentage points.

When the number of new voters in other States rose by only six to nineteen per cent over five years, why did it rise by twenty-nine per cent in Tamil Nadu alone? Were all of them genuine new voters? Or were large numbers of newly fabricated voters added under the name of voters? Would that not arouse suspicion?
Udanpirappē, to clear these doubts, while enrolling new voters you must also verify whether those already on the old roll genuinely exist.

Party headquarters published the enrolment schedule in Murasoli on 15 October 2014. Every Udanpirappu must treat this not merely as the work of office-bearers but as their own responsibility. With the assistance of those in charge, everyone must immediately enrol new voters and verify existing names. I will closely watch how this work is carried out in every district.

In every branch, you make strenuous efforts to contest internal party elections and report grievances about them to headquarters. Show no less effort, and indeed still greater enthusiasm, in adding new names to the electoral roll.

Udanpirappē, just as you take care to enrol new voters, carefully investigate whether those added last time were properly enrolled. Complaints say district election officers, especially District Collectors, receive applications from new voters but show little interest in removing those wrongly listed, or simply neglect the task.

The rolls reportedly contain the names of the dead, people who have moved elsewhere, people registered in two or three places, and single individuals enrolled under several names. It is said that such wrongful additions produced the 29.1 per cent increase between 2009 and 2014, and that votes cast by those unlawfully included gave one party an extraordinary parliamentary tally. That allegation appears to have a basis.

If names belonging to people who are not genuine voters appear on the roll, DMK Udanpirappugal must firmly work to place the details before the proper officer and have those false entries removed. If officials refuse petitions seeking removal, or fail to act, immediately inform party headquarters so that proper legal action can be taken.
I therefore ask every DMK Udanpirappu, including those wishing to contest the coming party elections, to make their contribution to this common work of the movement.

“Eternal vigilance is the price we pay for democracy.” It is not enough to focus only on victory. Only if we remain watchful and conscientious in preparing the electoral roll, the very foundation of that victory, will elections be conducted honestly and parliamentary democracy flourish without straying from its path.
With affection,
M.K.
